text: This case is a laparoscopic splenectomy in a canine patient. This was a five-year-old female husky referred for a history of lethargy and decreased appetite for about a month's duration. On abdominal ultrasound, she had a five centimeter heterogeneous splenic mass located in the tail of the spleen with no evidence of hemoabdomen and no evidence of metastatic disease. She had three view thoracic radiographs that were also clear of metastatic disease and the client elected laparoscopic splenectomy for her procedure.

The patient is positioned in dorsal recumbency, with the surgical area clipped widely for multiple trocar placements. Trochars are arranged in a semi-circle around the spleen, with the first trocar placed on the midline, caudal to the umbilicus. A modified Hassan technique is used to enter the abdomen, involving a skin incision and fascial stitches lateral to the linea alba. Hemostats are used to ensure safe abdominal entry before inserting the trocar and connecting insufflation tubing to achieve abdominal insufflation.

Additional ports are placed to triangulate visualization and instrument placement. The splenic mass is visible, and the surgeon uses the Asculap Cayman vessel sealing device to take down the falciform ligament, enhancing visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon elevates the spleen using a blunt instrument to avoid penetrating the capsule. The vessel sealing device is applied near the hilus to detach the connecting ligaments. The procedure begins at the caudal aspect of the spleen, progressing cranially to perform a hilar splenectomy, ensuring complete vessel sealing near the hilus.

An instrument retracts tissues away from the vessel sealing device to prevent thermal spread to nearby intestines or organs. The assistant uses a blunt instrument to retract the spleen. A vessel that was incompletely sealed and cut is grasped and occluded by the surgeon's instrument. The vessel sealing device is then applied to seal the vessel completely without cutting the remaining tissue.

The surgeon continues the splenectomy towards the head of the spleen, following along the hilus on the caudal aspect near the short gastric vessels. The camera port is adjusted to enhance visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a sterile camera sleeve as a catch bag for the spleen, with a pre-placed purse string suture of two O nylon securing the sleeve. Instruments hold the mouth of the sleeve open as the spleen, along with its associated mass, is carefully manipulated into the bag. The spleen is inserted mass first to minimize the risk of abdominal contamination in case of splenic capsule rupture. Once fully inserted, the drawstring is pulled to close the bag securely.

The surgeon pulls the drawstring through the trocar and out of the abdomen, enlarging the incision to approximately 2.5 centimeters to partially exteriorize the bag. The spleen is then morcellated, removing it in large pieces to facilitate histopathologic diagnosis and reduce morbidity. Large pieces of the spleen are visibly extracted using this method.

Blood is suctioned from inside the spleen to minimize contamination during the procedure. Once the spleen is sectioned into small pieces, the remaining tissue is removed along with the bag through a two and a half centimeter incision.

Her histopathologic diagnosis was a benign hematoma. We found for this case that using at least four ports was important for both visualization and the surgeon's ability to manipulate the spleen and surrounding tissue. It is ideal to place these ports in a semicircle around the tail of the spleen such that from any port you can access the hyalus. It also is important that the surgeon is able to hold two instruments one as the vessel sealing device and one as an additional grasper in the event that a vessel is damaged during the procedure as was shown here. It also can be important to recognize when to switch an instrument port to a camera port to allow a new perspective and view of the area of interest. And switching ports was done commonly for this procedure. Additionally, using the tilt table to use gravity and assistance for retraction and insertion of the spleen into the bag was incredibly helpful in this patient.

If you think that you have a pet or patient that qualifies for this or any laparoscopic procedure, you can contact myself, Dr. Elaine Holmes, at the Veterinary Specialty Hospital of the Carolinas in Cary, North Carolina.
